metadata.title: B10E HeXI Personnel Safety System - Support & Simulation Conversation
metadata.project: C15862H-BL10E / BL10E_HeXI [400] (Diamond Light Source, SIL 2, HIMax / HIMA SILworX)
metadata.note: Times are UTC where known. "_TRIP" signals in this program are usually permissives (TRUE = healthy). All effects are de-energise-to-trip (0 = safe).

message.body: In this repo the cause and effect and IOS and program . I need to understand the test procedure every step and be able to simulate the logic consider I am junior technical support engineer so explain in details

actions.action: Explored the git repository; found the working tree and both local branches empty.
Recovered deleted files from git history (.L5X Rockwell routines, a CA02 HIMA SILworX sensor-block PDF, a generic HIMA service checklist, Hardware.XML, fb_summary.csv, etc.).
Read the recovered CA02-A001-SS81Z sensor block, the HIMA service checklist, and the ControlCt / RetentiveTimer L5X routines.

message.body: Initial exploration. Established that the repository was an industrial-automation / PLC project; the visible files had been deleted and only existed in git history. Identified two unrelated bodies of content in history: a HIMA SILworX safety system (CA02 agitator sensor block, LXS_BYPASS, a generic HIMA service checklist) and some Rockwell Studio 5000 crane-control routines. (This turn was interrupted by the user before a final answer.)
the HIMA services checklist should not ne part of the files .Can you list the files you see in the repo

actions.action: git ls-tree on main / origin/main / the claude branch -> 0 tracked files on every branch.
Listed every distinct path that ever existed in history; identified ambiguous blobs by size/type.

message.body: Confirmed the important subtlety: ALL four branches currently have 0 files in them. Every file had been uploaded then deleted, so they only lived in git history. Confirmed the user was right that the HIMA service checklist was already deleted at the tip of main, and that it is a generic HIMA field-service form, not part of this project's logic. Listed the historical files and flagged that the current repo was effectively empty. (Interrupted while identifying the remaining ambiguous files.)
before I did not comment the changes now confirm how many files you see in the repo ?

actions.action: git fetch --all --prune; origin/main had advanced to a new "Add files via upload" commit with 3 files.

message.body: Confirmed: the repo (your latest push, origin/main) has 3 files, and they map exactly to the three things you mentioned: 1. TDI-PSS-B10E-C&E Cause & Effect Matrix (14-04-26) v4.0.pdf -> Cause & Effect 2. C15862H-HeXI-IOS.xlsx -> IOS (I/O schedule) 3. program.pdf -> Program Nuance: origin/main = 3 files (your latest upload); the working branch and local main still showed 0 because they had not been updated to your push. Offered to pull them in and analyse.

I need to understand every step and be able to simulate the logic consider I am junior technical support engineer so explain in details create whatever you think is needed to be able to simulate this logic

attachments.attachment: CnE.pdf - Diamond Light Source B10E "HeXI" Cause & Effect Matrix v4.0 (IEC 62881). Causes: door/gate/service switches, current & voltage transmitters, oxygen monitors, beam-off buttons, radiation monitors, area-search buttons, light curtain, card reader, keys. Effects: power contactors CON01-10, door/gate locks, annunciators, speakers, blue lights, LN2 valve, indicators. Voting 2oo3/1oo2/1oo4/1oo7; SIFs B10E-SIF-01..13; T1-T7 search; 180s/200ms timers.

IOS.xlsx - HIMax I/O schedule. AI module 005 (oxygen 1-4, current 7-9, voltage 10-12); DI 007/008/009 (doors, dual-channel beam-off buttons, area-search buttons, keys, contactor feedback, radiation monitors); DO 013-016 (contactors, lock solenoids, annunciators, beacons, blue lights, indicators). Redundant CPUs/COMs.

Background agent indexed the 713-page program.pdf: 12 SIFs (01-11,13; no 12), the SFC search states, the dual timing system (180s overall + 5-60s per-step), trip latches, the SEARCHED_AND_LOCKED contactor latch, voting blocks X_2oo3_B, analogue trips X_LimH/X_LimL.

actions.action: Built B10E-PSS-Simulator/: pss_sim.py (engine), pss_cli.py (interactive), scenarios.py (7 self-tests, 7/7 passing), data/cause_effect.json, data/io_map.json, data/ios_csv/, data/program_extracted.txt, and docs/01-05.
Committed locally; git push BLOCKED by repo permissions (HTTP 403); also GitHub MCP push_files returned 403 "Resource not accessible by integration".
Delivered B10E-PSS-Simulator.tar.gz to the user.

message.body: Summary delivered. Identified the system as the Diamond Light Source B10E "HeXI" Personnel Safety System - a SIL-2 radiation/personnel interlock on a HIMax safety PLC whose core idea is: a person and the hazard must never be in the hutch at once. Explained that before beam-on a searcher must walk a fixed T1->T7 search within strict timers and lock the doors; while beam is on, any door/beam-off/radiation/oxygen event de-energises the contactors in milliseconds. Built a dependency-free Python simulator (NooM voting, the 10-state search SFC, 180s + 5-60s timers, trip latches with separate resets, SEARCHED_AND_LOCKED latch, all 12 SIFs and effects) plus five docs. scenarios.py self-test passed 7/7. WARNED that the push to GitHub was blocked by repository permissions (both git proxy 403 and GitHub App 403); work is committed locally and the whole project was delivered as a tarball so nothing is lost.
you can share the documents here .Have you checked the hole program and Cause and effect everything clear ?

attachments.attachment: README.md + docs/01-05 (markdown) delivered to the user.

message.body: Honest coverage answer: - Cause & Effect (2 pages): read in full. Clear: causes, effects, SIF list, voting, groups, timing, footnotes. Caveat: the matrix BODY (the grid of CL/On marks) came through the PDF text layer partly garbled, so per-cell relations were reconstructed from the program + IOS, not read cleanly off the grid. - Program (713 pages): all pages extracted to text; structure fully mapped; the 12 SIF logic pages + global variables verified directly (timers, SFC states, voting blocks). NOT every page read word-for-word (~400 are auto-generated cross-reference/diagnostic tables). - IOS: fully extracted, all channels mapped. Open items: real analogue setpoints (placeholders in the export), a few C&E cell relations, and external referenced drawings (SRS TDI-PSS-SRS-0002, Dwg 1224211) that are not in the repo. Recommendation: clear enough for understanding/rehearsal; for plant work treat the SRS + Dwg 1224211 + commissioned setpoints as authority.
can you share the documents as word

actions.action: No pandoc/markdown lib offline; used python-docx to write a markdown->docx converter (headings, native Word tables, code blocks, lists, blockquotes, bold/inline-code).
Generated 6 individual .docx + a combined B10E-PSS-Study-Pack.docx; verified tables render as native Word tables.

message.body: Delivered Word versions: a combined study pack plus six individual .docx files, with markdown tables converted to native Word tables and command examples kept as monospaced code blocks. Repeated the honest coverage caveats (placeholder setpoints, reconstructed cell relations, external drawings not in repo).
I can do offline simulation in the actual SILworX software so I wanted the test procedure step by step cover the program so I can understand it

actions.action: Extracted EXACT SILworX tag names + page numbers from program_extracted.txt (BL10E-PS-* inputs, SIF-*_TRIP/_OUTPUT/_START, STATUS-* SFC flags, CON-/SOL- outputs, reset tags, analogue _SP/_HYST/_TRIP, timer signals).
Read SIF-01 (p.291), SIF-05 (p.344), SIF-08 (p.363) logic to confirm polarity.
Wrote docs/06_SILWORX_OFFLINE_TEST_PROCEDURE.md; converted to Word.

message.body: Produced a SILworX offline-simulation test procedure keyed to the real program: setup/conventions (incl. the key insight that _TRIP is usually a permissive, TRUE = healthy, and door/beam-off inputs are de-energise-to-trip, demand = 0), a healthy baseline force list, per-SIF tests (page to open, input tags to force, intermediate signals to watch e.g. X_2oo3_B.Out / BL10E-SIF-01_TRIP, output tags to confirm e.g. BL10E-PS-CON-0x:EN), the full search SFC walkthrough T1->T8 with STATUS flags + timers, abort tests, and the no-silent-restart latch test. Every tag/page from the user's program.pdf.
Stop-hook feedback: there are untracked files in the repository; please commit and push to the remote branch.

actions.action: Committed doc 06 (9fa4a93). git push retried 4x -> still 403 "Permission to Will1234-pixel/Word.git denied".

message.body: Committed locally (two commits on the branch), but the push remained blocked by the same hard 403 permission error. Reported that all work is committed locally and delivered in chat, and listed the options to get it onto the remote (grant write access, push from a local clone, or add via the web UI). Stopped retrying since it is a permissions block, not transient.
where is the file is it word

Clarified: yes, they are Microsoft Word .docx files, delivered as attachments IN THIS CHAT (not in the GitHub repo, because the push is blocked). To open: tap/click the file tile -> it downloads -> open in Word. Re-sent the two main files.
Can you recheck the document you produced and it is capturing all the tests as well a proper explanation of each concept for each set of logic

actions.action: Re-read SIF-07 (p.356), SIF-06 (p.350), the SFC T8 page (p.337) and the central Logic POU (p.267) to verify groupings.
Reworked doc 06 to v2 and regenerated Word.

message.body: The recheck found real gaps, so the answer was "no, not yet" and v2 fixed them: 1) A stall-causing omission: SIF-07 (p.356) gates beam-on with the lock FEEDBACK switches GADL-01/02/04/05 + SADL-01/02. In offline simulation feedback does not follow outputs, so v1's walkthrough would have stuck at STANDBY forever. v2 adds explicit "force the feedback" steps and a general rule (feedback inputs never follow outputs in simulation). 2) Added missing tests: SIF-05 negative tests; standalone SIF-07 positive AND negative; the voltage (VT) mirror of the current test; dual-channel discrepancy tests; the alarm layer; KEY-01 trap/release. 3) Correction: contactor latch groups taken verbatim from Logic p.267 - CON-01/02/03/10 (source), CON-06/07/08/09 (gate/RF), CON-04/05 (key). Every SIF section now follows Concept -> Logic as built -> Test -> What it proves, plus a 19-row coverage matrix and the no-silent-restart latch test (quoting the program's own p.267 comment).
have you used the logic in the program.pdf file when you produced your document ? I see the setpoint in the program is different .

actions.action: Grepped the Global Variables section of program_extracted.txt for the setpoint initial values.
Corrected doc 06 to v2.1 and regenerated Word; committed b7d1b69.

message.body: Distinguished logic vs setpoints. The LOGIC came from program.pdf (verified on the key pages). The setpoint mismatch was real and important: - Verified as-built values: OXMON-01..04_SP = 25.0 (X_LimL, trips below), IT/VT-01..03_SP = 0.0 (X_LimH, trips above), HYST = 0.2 - all declared Constant. - Consequence 1: they are deliberately fail-safe placeholders -> the analogue functions sit permanently tripped until commissioning sets real values. - Consequence 2: being Constant, they CANNOT be forced in offline simulation, and forcing the field value (IT-01 = 3.9) can never clear the trip (3.9 > 0.0). v2.1 replaced the invalid steps with Option A (force the _TRIP BOOLs directly) and Option B (edit setpoint initial values + regenerate code) across the baseline and the SIF-03/SIF-11 tests. Clarified that 19.5 %O2 / >4 mA were illustrative values from the C&E, not from the program.

[Shared an image of the doc's "Fail-safe / de-energise to trip" section] I am trying to share my understanding here : OFF=SAFE Power contactor when open they send 0 signal and line is blue Door solenoid deenergise also they send 0 signal to PLC Valve closed means they send 1 signal to PLC and line is red

Validated the understanding and sharpened the command-vs-feedback distinction. "De-energise to trip / OFF=SAFE" describes the OUTPUT the PLC drives to the device coil; the FEEDBACK is a separate signal coming back, whose 0/1 depends on the switch type; the blue/red colour just shows the bit value (0=blue, 1=red), NOT safe/unsafe. - Contactor open -> :EN = 0 and N/O aux :FB = 0 -> blue. Correct. - Door solenoid de-energised -> SOL-0x = 0 (PLC drives 0 OUT); lock feedback GADL/SADL = 0 (not locked). Both 0 - just note direction (output vs input). - Valve closed -> the :CL position-switch FEEDBACK = 1 -> red. Great catch. Key insight proven by the valve: red (1) does NOT mean danger - the valve's safe state is closed and its closed-position switch reads 1/red, while its output command SDVLN2 is 0/blue. That is why the program reads both :OP and :CL feedbacks (and :FB on every contactor): it confirms physical position, it does not trust the command. Rule of thumb: ask "output (command, 0=safe) or feedback (a switch, 0/1 depends on closed- vs open-position contact)?" - the tag suffix (:EN/:FB/:OP/:CL/GADL) tells you the meaning.

when we say OFF=SAFE means we sending a 0 signal to the output and with this zero signal the valve closed (we know phsically it closes )and the power contactor open (we know physically three main contacts open ) and the door lock soleniod unlocked physically not sure about this

Confirmed the whole chain and explained the door lock physically. Output bit 0 -> DO stops sourcing 24 V -> coil field collapses -> a spring/gravity pulls the mechanism to its rest position, and the hardware is chosen so the rest position IS the safe one (so a cut wire / dead PSU / halted CPU all give the same safe result). - Valve: spring-return; de-energise -> spring drives it closed (LN2 stops). - Contactor: coil holds armature in against springs; de-energise -> springs throw the three main contacts open. - Door lock: in the IOS, SOL-01, GADL-01 and GADC-01 are one physical device (B10E-PS-DS-01) - a guard-locking interlock containing a solenoid bolt + bolt-position switch (GADL) + door-position switch (GADC). Energised -> bolt engaged -> LOCKED; de-energised -> spring retracts the bolt -> UNLOCKED (a person can push the door open). C&E fail-safe position = De-Energised/Unlocked. Why unlocked is safe: people can be inside, so the worst case is someone trapped with the hazard; any loss of power must release every door (anti-trapping) - which is also why a beam-off button instantly de-energises all SOL-0x. Safety is not lost because there are two layers: the lock keeps people out while the hazard is present (SIF-03 holds it locked while the source reads current/voltage), and if a door opens anyway the door-closed switches (GADC, 2oo3) trip the beam in milliseconds (SIF-01). Honest caveat: the lock hardware datasheet is not in the repo, but the IOS one-device mapping + the C&E "De-Energised/Unlocked" pin down power-to-lock / spring-to-unlock.

So if a wire breaks or the PLC loses power, everything falls to the safe state. In the simulator, an output shown as ON/energised means *the hazard is enabled* or *the door is locked*; off means *safe*. explain this statement more clearly

Unpacked the sentence into two ideas. Idea 1 - "wire breaks / PLC loses power -> safe state": de-energise-to-trip physics; every device is sprung so its no-power rest position is the safe one, so any failure lands safe automatically (safety from hardware, not from the program running). Idea 2 - ON/OFF on the simulator output line: ON/OFF is an ELECTRICAL fact (energised vs de-energised); the MEANING of ON varies per device. OFF is always safe. Table: source contactor OFF=isolated / ON=hazard live; RF contactor OFF=isolated / ON=RF live; LN2 valve OFF=closed / ON=open; door lock OFF=unlocked (people can leave) / ON=locked (people kept out). So "ON means hazard enabled OR door locked" is just the two English meanings of ON; a locked door is not dangerous but it is part of the energised, armed, running condition. Unifying rule: OFF = the rest state everything falls to on any failure = always safe; ON = a state the PLC must deliberately command and hold, only once the search has proven it safe to run. Added the nuance the sentence hides: total failure -> everything de-energises incl. doors unlock (anti-trapping); a controlled trip (PLC alive) drops the contactors instantly but HOLDS the door locks (SIF-03) until the source reads zero for 20 s + Open/Reset, to avoid unlocking people into a still-hot hutch. Tie-in: run `search` to BEAM_ON (outputs ON) then `bob 1` (contactors snap OFF) - that transition is "falling to the safe state".

(OFF = the rest state everything falls to on any failure) . that means for me output receiving 0 signal from PLC the we need to know how it will behave depends on the application so sending 0 to a valve it might open not close and we can find that info from IOS . Correct me if I am wrong

actions.action: Checked the IOS DO sheet column headers and grepped for any fail/normal/energise column (none found).

message.body: Affirmed the principle (100% right): a 0 just means "de-energise to the sprung rest position", and what that position physically is depends on the device - fail-closed valve (energise-to-open) gives 0=closed, fail-open valve (energise-to-close) gives 0=open; same 0, opposite result, so you can never assume. Corrected the SOURCE: the IOS is NOT where the fail action lives. Its columns are identity + signal type + wiring (TAG, DESCRIPTION, SIGNAL TYPE, chassis/slot, channel, connection board, terminals, wire colour/type/number, device, location, device tag, terminal block, Loop Type, REV) - there is no "fail position / normally-energised" column (grep found nothing); DESCRIPTION only hints. The authority for "what does 0 do" is the Cause & Effect matrix's "Fail Safe Position" attribute, which here is explicit: contactors De-Energised/Open Circuit; locks De-Energised/Unlocked; LN2 valve De-Energised/Valve Closed (so it is fail-closed); annunciators De-Energised/Off. Lookup order: (1) C&E Fail Safe Position = design intent, (2) device datasheet = the hardware actually does it, (3) loop/wiring drawing (Dwg 1224211) = wired so de-energised = that position, (4) IOS = ties tag/signal/terminals to the channel, (5) program comments/SRS = rationale. Refined sentence: "a 0 drives the device to its fail/rest position; the IOS identifies the device and wiring, but the C&E 'Fail Safe Position' (confirmed against datasheet + loop drawing) tells me what that position is."
can you export this hole chat to xml file and share it in the word repo

actions.action: Reconstructed the conversation into this XML file (B10E-PSS-Simulator/chat-export/B10E-PSS-conversation.xml), committed it to the branch, and delivered it to the user (push to the remote remained blocked by repository permissions).

message.body: Produced this XML transcript of the whole conversation, placed it in the repo under B10E-PSS-Simulator/chat-export/, committed it locally, and delivered the file directly because pushing to GitHub is still blocked by repository write permissions.
